$description: The attention gradient maps emphasis levels to visual treatment across the design system. It answers: 'How prominent should this element be?' Every element exists somewhere on this gradient, from interactive (demands attention) to subliminal (felt, not seen). The gradient uses gold opacity as its primary expression, but also governs size, weight, spacing, and motion.

interactive.purpose: The seeker is actively engaging. This element demands and deserves full attention.

prominent.purpose: Important but not interactive. Draws the eye without requiring action.

decorative.purpose: Beautiful without calling. Background beauty that enriches without distracting.

treatment.size: large (decorative elements are often larger but visually lighter)

ambient.purpose: Peripheral awareness. Orientation without distraction — the seeker knows where they are without looking directly.

highlight.purpose: Guiding without grabbing. Showing the seeker where they are in the text.

subliminal.purpose: Felt, not seen. Registers as warmth or presence, not as a visible color.

treatment.motion: interaction speed (150ms) — fast because it shouldn't be noticed transitioning

texture.purpose: The ghost of physicality. Present only as a feeling — 'this doesn't feel like a screen.'
elements: paper texture overlay on reading surfaces

secondary-text-hierarchy.$description: Text opacity levels for non-primary text. Follows the same gradient principle.

tertiary.use: Placeholder text, disabled labels, hint text

border-hierarchy.$description: Border visibility follows the attention gradient. Most boundaries in this design language are implied (whitespace, background shifts), not drawn.

prominent.use: Section dividers, card borders on hover

subtle.use: Internal dividers within a card, table rows

none.use: Reading surfaces, clean content areas. Preferred.
